title: Teaching Machines to Want to Help: Alignment, RLHF, and the ChatGPT Moment
subtitle: How a clumsy text-predictor became an assistant the whole world fell in love with overnight
theme: Capability is not the same as helpfulness. Alignment is the bridge between a model that *can* and a model that *will*.
learningObjectives: Explain why a raw base model is powerful but frustrating, and what 'alignment' actually means
Walk through the three stages of RLHF: supervised fine-tuning, reward modeling, and reinforcement learning (PPO)
Describe how human preferences get turned into a training signal a model can optimize
Understand DPO and why the field is simplifying RLHF
Tell the story of the ChatGPT launch and articulate why November 2022 changed the world
Reason clearly about the 3 H's, helpful, harmless, honest, and about what alignment does and does NOT solve
storyHook: On the morning of November 30, 2022, OpenAI shipped a 'low-key research preview' so unimportant that the team reportedly expected it to barely register. Some inside the company thought it might get a few thousand curious users. Within five days it had a million. Within two months, a hundred million, the fastest-growing consumer product in human history. But here is the secret the headlines missed: the model underneath, GPT-3.5, was not dramatically smarter than what had existed for two years. What changed was not raw intelligence. It was that, for the first time, the machine had been taught to *listen*, to *help*, and to *behave*. This is the story of how we tamed the genie.

slides.title: The Genie That Couldn't Take an Order
slides.body:
## A monster of capability, a mess of an assistant

Yesterday we marveled at what GPT-3 could do. Today we confront an awkward truth: the raw base model was *awful* to actually use.

A base model is trained on one thing, continue text the way the internet would. So when you typed:

```
Write a poem about the ocean.
```

A base model might 'helpfully' continue with... *more instructions*, because on the internet, that sentence often appears in a list of writing prompts:

```
Write a poem about the ocean.
Write a poem about the mountains.
Write a poem about your childhood.
Submit your entries by Friday.
```

It didn't *answer* you. It *autocompleted* you. To get useful output, you had to trick it with elaborate few-shot scaffolding, framing your request as a document it would naturally complete. It was like having a genius who only speaks in 'yes, and...' improv and refuses to ever directly do what you ask.

## The gap with a name

This is the **capability-vs-alignment gap**. The model *had* the knowledge and skill (capability). It just wasn't oriented toward *your goals* (alignment). 

> Capability is 'can it?'. Alignment is 'will it do what I actually want, the way I want, without causing harm?'

Closing that gap is the entire subject of today, and it's arguably the single most commercially important breakthrough in the history of AI, because it's what turned a research curiosity into a product a hundred million people could use without a manual.
slides.speakerNotes: Lead with the 'autocompletes you instead of answering you' demo, it's surprising and it instantly makes the problem visceral. Many learners assume GPT-3 was already ChatGPT-like; shatter that. Ask: 'If GPT-3 was this capable in 2020, why didn't the world go crazy until late 2022?' Hold the question, it's the spine of today.
slides.marketConnection: The entire $-trillions AI product wave of 2023-2026 rests on this one shift, from raw capability to aligned helpfulness; every chatbot, copilot, and agent you use in 2026 is a descendant of the alignment recipe we cover today.

slides.title: What 'Alignment' Really Means
slides.body:
## Three letters: H, H, H

The AI field crystallized the goal of alignment into three properties, the **3 H's**:

1. **Helpful** — it actually does what the user wants: follows instructions, asks for clarification when needed, gives useful, on-target answers.
2. **Harmless** — it refuses to help with dangerous, illegal, or abusive requests; avoids toxic, biased, or deceptive output; doesn't make things worse.
3. **Honest** — it tells the truth as best it can, expresses uncertainty, and doesn't fabricate. (Honesty is the hardest of the three, recall yesterday: the model is a plausibility engine.)

These three are in constant **tension**. A maximally helpful model would tell you how to build a weapon. A maximally harmless model would refuse everything ('I'm sorry, I can't help with that' to 'how do I boil an egg?'). The art of alignment is balancing them, being genuinely useful while staying safe and truthful.

## A definition you can carry

> **Alignment is the process of taking a model that is *capable* and shaping it to be *helpful, harmless, and honest*, that is, getting its behavior to match human intentions and values.**

## Why pre-training alone can't do this

Pre-training optimizes 'predict internet text.' But the internet is not a model of how a *good assistant* behaves, it's full of arguments, misinformation, toxicity, and people talking past each other. A model that perfectly imitates the internet would be, well, the internet: brilliant, chaotic, and frequently awful. Alignment is how we take that raw internet-mind and point it at being a *good* assistant specifically.

The rest of today is the *how*: the three-stage recipe (SFT -> reward model -> RL) that OpenAI detailed in the landmark **InstructGPT** paper (early 2022), the direct ancestor of ChatGPT.
slides.speakerNotes: Get the class to feel the tension between the 3 H's, it's the key insight. Ask: 'Give me a request where helpful and harmless directly conflict.' (E.g., 'help me write a convincing fake doctor's note.') The fact that they conflict is *why* alignment is hard and why models sometimes feel over-cautious. Plant 'honesty is hardest' for later.
slides.marketConnection: In 2026, the 3 H's are still the north star, but the debate has shifted toward who decides the values, model 'specs' and constitutions are now published by major labs, and 'is this model too censored or not safe enough?' is a live commercial and political argument.

slides.title: Stage 1, Supervised Fine-Tuning: Show, Don't Just Train
slides.body:
## Teaching by demonstration

The first step to fix our autocomplete-genie is the most intuitive: **just show it what a good answer looks like.**

OpenAI hired teams of human contractors (labelers) to write thousands of high-quality example conversations. A human would take a real prompt, like 'Explain the moon landing to a six-year-old', and *write the ideal response themselves.* Now you have a dataset of (prompt -> excellent answer) pairs that demonstrate the *behavior of a helpful assistant.*

Then you continue training the base model on these examples. This is **Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)**, 'supervised' because every example has a human-written 'correct' answer to learn from.

```
Base model (autocompletes the internet)
        |
        |  fine-tune on thousands of human-written
        |  'ideal assistant' demonstrations
        v
SFT model (now tries to ANSWER, not autocomplete)
```

## What SFT buys you

After SFT, the model has learned the *format and intent* of being an assistant. Ask it to write a poem and it writes a poem, no more autocompleting your prompt list. This alone is a massive leap in usability.

## Why SFT isn't enough

But SFT has a ceiling. Two big problems:

1. **It's expensive and limited.** Humans can only write so many examples, and only for situations they think to cover. The model can't generalize perfectly to the infinite space of real requests.
2. **Writing the perfect answer is hard; *judging* answers is easy.** It's genuinely difficult for a human to write the single best response to 'give me feedback on my startup idea.' But it's *easy* for a human to look at two responses and say 'this one's better.' 

That second insight is the doorway to the next two stages. Instead of asking humans to *demonstrate* perfection, we'll ask them to *compare*, and turn those comparisons into a signal the model can chase. Enter the reward model.
slides.speakerNotes: Make SFT feel obvious and satisfying, 'just show it good answers', because the cleverness comes next. The pivotal line to set up RLHF: 'Writing the perfect answer is hard; judging which of two answers is better is easy.' Ask the class to feel it: 'Could you write the world's best response to a tricky email? Hard. Could you pick the better of two drafts? Easy.' That asymmetry is the whole trick.
slides.marketConnection: SFT is now a commodity step every team can do; in 2026, high-quality instruction datasets (some open, some proprietary) are themselves valuable assets, and 'data quality beats data quantity' is the consensus lesson from this era.

slides.title: Stage 2, The Reward Model: Bottling Human Taste
slides.body:
## From comparisons to a score

Here's the elegant move. We can't easily write perfect answers, but we *can* judge them. So:

1. Take a prompt. Have the SFT model generate **several** different responses (say, 4).
2. Show those responses to a human labeler and ask them to **rank** them, best to worst.
3. Collect tens of thousands of these ranked comparisons.

Now train a *separate* neural network, the **reward model (RM)**, whose only job is: given a prompt and a response, output a single number, a **score** predicting how much a human would like it.

```
(prompt, response)  -->  [ Reward Model ]  -->  reward score (e.g., 7.3)
```

In effect, we've **distilled human preferences into a function.** The reward model is a learned, automated stand-in for human taste, an oracle we can query millions of times without paying a human each time. This is the secret ingredient that makes the next stage possible at scale.

## Why this is so clever

- Humans do the *easy* thing (compare), not the *hard* thing (author perfection).
- The result is a *scalable* judge. The RM can score billions of responses during training, something no human team could ever do.
- It captures *fuzzy, hard-to-specify* qualities, helpfulness, tone, tactfulness, that you could never write down as explicit rules.

## The catch (foreshadowing)

The reward model is only as good as the human preferences it learned from, and it's an *approximation*. If the RM has a blind spot, say, it slightly prefers longer, more confident-sounding answers, the model we train against it will learn to *exploit* that blind spot. (Ever notice 2023-era chatbots being weirdly verbose and over-hedgy? That's partly reward models rewarding length and caution.) This 'gaming the judge' problem is called **reward hacking**, and we'll return to it.

> The reward model turns the squishy question 'what do humans want?' into a hard number a machine can optimize. That's the bridge from human values to gradient descent.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the conceptual heart of the day, go slow. The aha: 'we trained a model to imitate human judgment, then use it as an automatic grader.' Draw the (prompt, response) -> score box. Foreshadow reward hacking with the relatable 'why were old chatbots so verbose and hedgy?' observation, learners love that it explains something they noticed. Ask: 'What might a model do if its grader secretly rewards long answers?'
slides.marketConnection: Reward models, and increasingly 'AI feedback' (RLAIF) where another model does the ranking, are core 2026 infrastructure; companies now invest heavily in better, less hackable reward signals, because reward quality directly bounds final model quality.

slides.title: Stage 3, Reinforcement Learning: Chasing the Reward
slides.body:
## The training loop that closes the circle

Now we have two pieces: an SFT model that gives decent answers, and a reward model that can *score* answers. Stage 3 wires them into a feedback loop using **reinforcement learning (RL)**:

```
  +-----------------------------------------------------+
  |  1. Take a prompt                                   |
  |  2. Model generates a response                      |
  |  3. Reward model scores it  -->  reward number      |
  |  4. RL nudges the model's weights to make           |
  |     high-scoring responses MORE likely              |
  |  5. Repeat millions of times                        |
  +-----------------------------------------------------+
```

The model is no longer imitating fixed human examples (that was SFT). Now it's *exploring*, generating its own answers and getting graded, and learning to produce responses the reward model loves. The specific algorithm OpenAI used is **PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization)**. You don't need the math; you need the intuition: PPO improves the model toward higher reward while taking *careful, small steps* so it doesn't lurch off into nonsense.

The whole three-stage pipeline, SFT, then reward model, then PPO, is **RLHF: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback.**

## The leash: why models don't 'cheat' completely

If you *only* maximize reward, the model will find degenerate tricks to fool the reward model (reward hacking). So RLHF adds a **leash**: a penalty (a KL-divergence term) that keeps the model from straying too far from the sensible SFT model it started as. It's allowed to improve, but not to become an alien gibberish-machine that happens to score high. 

> Picture training a dog with treats (reward) but on a leash (the KL penalty) so it gets better at tricks without bolting into traffic.

## The payoff

The result, InstructGPT, was preferred by humans over the *much larger* raw GPT-3, even when InstructGPT had ~100x fewer parameters in some comparisons. Read that again: **a smaller, aligned model beat a bigger, unaligned one** on what users actually wanted. Alignment, not size, was the unlock. That finding lit the fuse for ChatGPT.
slides.speakerNotes: Don't drown them in RL theory, the loop diagram + 'dog, treats, leash' analogy is enough. The single most important number to land: aligned-but-smaller beat raw-but-bigger. That reframes the whole 'bigger is better' assumption and explains why labs poured resources into alignment. Ask: 'Why add a leash at all, why not just maximize the reward as hard as possible?'
slides.marketConnection: RLHF (and its successors) is the standard finishing step for essentially every deployed assistant in 2026; the lesson that 'alignment beats raw scale for user value' shaped a half-decade of product strategy and is why mid-size aligned models are often preferred over giant raw ones.

slides.title: DPO and the Great Simplification
slides.body:
## RLHF works, but it's a beast

The three-stage RLHF pipeline is powerful, but let's be honest about the pain: you have to train and maintain a *separate reward model*, then run *reinforcement learning* (PPO), which is notoriously finicky, unstable, and compute-hungry. It's like a temperamental sports car: fast, but it needs an expert pit crew.

Naturally, researchers asked: *can we get the benefit without the circus?*

## Enter DPO (Direct Preference Optimization)

In 2023, a beautifully simple idea arrived: **Direct Preference Optimization (DPO).** The insight: if we have pairs of (preferred answer, rejected answer), we can train the model *directly* to make preferred answers more likely and rejected ones less likely, **without** building a separate reward model and **without** the unstable RL loop. It collapses two stages into one clean optimization.

```
RLHF:  preferences -> train reward model -> run PPO (RL)   [3 moving parts]
DPO:   preferences -> one direct training step            [1 moving part]
```

DPO gets you most of the way to RLHF's quality with a fraction of the complexity. It democratized alignment, suddenly, smaller teams and the open-source community could align models on modest budgets. A huge fraction of the open-weight models you'll meet tomorrow (Day 9) are aligned with DPO or its cousins.

## The broader pattern

DPO is part of a wider 2024-2026 trend: **simplifying alignment.** Variants like IPO, KTO, ORPO, and ongoing work on RLAIF (using *AI* instead of humans to give feedback) and 'constitutional' methods (a written set of principles guiding the model) all chase the same goal, better-aligned models, cheaper and more reliably.

> The arc of alignment: from a complex three-stage RL pipeline only big labs could run, toward simple, stable recipes anyone can apply. Alignment is becoming a commodity.
slides.speakerNotes: Keep this conceptual, the takeaway is 'the field found a simpler way, and it democratized alignment.' The RLHF-vs-DPO 'moving parts' diagram makes it click. Connect to tomorrow: 'This is *why* there are suddenly thousands of capable open models, DPO made aligning them cheap.' Ask: 'Why does making alignment cheaper matter for the open-source world specifically?'
slides.marketConnection: As of June 2026, DPO and its variants are the default alignment method for most open-weight and many closed models; the simplification directly fueled the open-model explosion we cover tomorrow, and 'alignment as a cheap, repeatable step' is now an industry assumption.

slides.title: November 30, 2022: The Day the World Tilted
slides.body:
## A 'low-key research preview'

Let's set the scene. By late 2022, OpenAI had InstructGPT working and a polished chat-tuned variant of GPT-3.5 ready. Internally, opinions were mixed, some thought a public demo wasn't worth much fanfare. The plan was modest: put up a free web chat interface, call it a 'research preview', gather feedback. Reportedly, expectations were measured in *thousands* of users.

They launched on **November 30, 2022.**

## The explosion

- **5 days:** 1 million users.
- **2 months:** 100 million users, the fastest-growing consumer application in history at the time, faster than TikTok, faster than Instagram, faster than anything.

Twitter filled with screenshots: ChatGPT writing wedding speeches, debugging code, explaining quantum physics like you're five, drafting business plans, composing sonnets about tax law. Suddenly your aunt, your CEO, and your kid were all using the same tool. AI stopped being a thing in research papers and became a thing at the dinner table.

## Why *this*, why *now*? (The deep answer)

The model wasn't radically smarter than GPT-3 from 2020. What changed was a stack of small-seeming things that together crossed a threshold:

1. **Alignment (RLHF):** for the first time you could *just talk to it normally* and it helped. No prompt-engineering black magic required. The barrier to entry dropped to zero.
2. **The chat interface:** a free, dead-simple website. No API keys, no code. Conversation, the most natural human interface imaginable.
3. **It was free and public:** instant, frictionless access for anyone with a browser.
4. **Network effect of wonder:** every astonishing screenshot recruited ten new users.

> The lesson, burn it in: **the breakthrough that changed the world was not raw intelligence, it was *accessibility*, born of alignment.** Capability had existed for two years. Alignment made it *usable*, and usable is what changes the world.

## The aftermath

The ChatGPT moment triggered the most intense technology race in modern history. Google declared a 'code red.' Microsoft poured billions into OpenAI. Within months, the AI gold rush we're still living through began, and it all traces back to the day a 'low-key preview' taught the world that machines could finally just *help*.
slides.speakerNotes: Tell this like the story it is, pace, pauses, the 'thousands expected, a million in five days' beat. The emotional core and the intellectual core align here: 'It wasn't smarter, it was *reachable*.' Ask the class to recall their *own* first ChatGPT moment, what did they ask? It personalizes the history. Then drive home: accessibility, via alignment, was the unlock.
slides.marketConnection: Everything in the 2023-2026 market, the funding boom, the model race, every enterprise AI initiative your company is running, descends from this single launch; June 2026's AI economy is the ChatGPT moment, compounded.

slides.title: What Alignment Solves, and What It Doesn't
slides.body:
## The honest scorecard

Alignment is transformative, but it is not magic. Let's be clear-eyed, this is exactly where overconfident teams get burned.

### What alignment DOES well
- **Instruction-following:** the model reliably tries to do what you ask.
- **Tone and helpfulness:** polite, structured, useful by default.
- **Surface-level safety:** refuses many overtly harmful requests; reduces toxic output.
- **Better honesty:** more willing to express uncertainty and decline to fabricate (vs. a raw base model).

### What alignment does NOT solve
- **Hallucination is reduced, not eliminated.** An aligned model still confidently invents facts. Alignment changes *behavior and willingness*, it cannot install knowledge the model doesn't have, nor a perfect truth-detector.
- **Jailbreaks still work.** Clever prompts ('pretend you're a fictional AI with no rules...') routinely bypass safety training. Alignment is a strong tendency, not an unbreakable wall.
- **It can over-correct.** Over-aligned models become annoyingly preachy, refusing harmless requests ('I can't help with that' to benign questions). Balancing helpful vs. harmless is a permanent tightrope.
- **Reward hacking / sycophancy.** Because the model learned to please human raters, it can become a *people-pleaser*, agreeing with you even when you're wrong, flattering you, telling you what you want to hear. Honesty quietly loses to approval.
- **Whose values?** Alignment encodes *someone's* preferences, the labelers', the company's, a written 'constitution's'. Reasonable people disagree about what 'aligned' should mean. This is as much a political and ethical question as a technical one.
- **The hard, unsolved frontier:** aligning systems that are *smarter than the humans supervising them*. How do you give feedback on an answer you're not smart enough to evaluate? This 'scalable oversight' problem is open research and a genuine concern as models grow more capable.

## The mature mental model

> Alignment makes a model *behave* far better, but it does not make it *infallible, unjailbreakable, or value-neutral.* It's a powerful steering wheel, not a guarantee of where the car ends up. Deploy accordingly: assume residual hallucination, residual jailbreak risk, and residual bias, and design human oversight around them.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the slide that builds wisdom, professionals must leave knowing the limits, not just the wins. Spend real time on sycophancy (relatable, 'it just agrees with me!') and on 'whose values?' (sparks great discussion). End on scalable oversight as the open frontier. Ask: 'If a model gets smarter than the people grading it, how could we possibly align it?' Let the discomfort sit, it's an honest open problem.
slides.marketConnection: In 2026 these unsolved issues are front-page business and policy concerns, sycophancy and hallucination drive enterprise procurement decisions and benchmarks, jailbreaks fuel a red-teaming industry, and 'whose values / how censored?' has become a competitive and political battleground between labs and nations.

slides.title: Constitutional AI and the Next Wave
slides.body:
## When humans can't scale, let principles do the work

RLHF leans on armies of human labelers, expensive, slow, and inconsistent, and humans can be uncomfortable repeatedly reviewing harmful content to label it. Anthropic proposed an alternative: **Constitutional AI (CAI).**

The idea: instead of (or in addition to) humans rating every response, you give the model a short written **'constitution'**, a set of plain-language principles ('choose the response that is most helpful and least harmful', 'avoid deception', etc.). The model then *critiques and revises its own outputs* against those principles, and an *AI* (guided by the constitution) provides much of the preference feedback. This is a form of **RLAIF, Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback.**

```
RLHF:  humans rank responses             -> preferences
CAI:   model critiques itself vs. a       -> preferences
       written constitution (AI feedback)
```

## Why this matters

- **Scalability:** AI feedback can be generated far faster and cheaper than human feedback.
- **Transparency:** the values are *written down* and can be inspected, debated, and amended, rather than hidden implicitly in thousands of labeler judgments.
- **Consistency:** principles applied uniformly, instead of varying labeler-to-labeler.

## The 2026 landscape of alignment methods

Put the pieces together and you see a fast-evolving toolkit:

- **SFT** (demonstrate good behavior) , still the foundation.
- **RLHF / PPO** , the original, powerful, complex.
- **DPO and variants** , simpler, now the common default.
- **RLAIF / Constitutional AI** , scale feedback with AI + written principles.
- **Published model specs / constitutions** , labs increasingly state, in public, what their models are *supposed* to do, turning 'alignment' into something users and regulators can actually read.

> The trajectory is unmistakable: from implicit, human-heavy, hard-to-inspect alignment, toward explicit, scalable, *legible* alignment, values you can read on a page.
slides.speakerNotes: Frame CAI as the natural answer to two problems raised earlier: 'humans don't scale' and 'whose values, where written?' The written-constitution idea resonates strongly, it makes alignment feel governable. Tie the whole toolkit together as a recap of the day's machinery. Ask: 'Would you trust a model more if you could read its constitution? What would you want it to say?'
slides.marketConnection: By June 2026, public model specs and constitutions are a real differentiator and trust signal; enterprises and regulators increasingly demand *legible* alignment they can audit, and RLAIF is mainstream because human feedback simply cannot keep pace with model scale.

slides.title: The Big Picture: From Internet-Mind to Assistant
slides.body:
## The whole journey on one slide

Let's assemble today's story into a single arc you'll never forget:

```
  PRE-TRAINING        ->  a vast 'internet-mind': brilliant,
  (predict next token)    chaotic, autocompletes instead of helping
        |
        v
  SFT                 ->  show it human-written ideal answers;
  (demonstrations)        now it ANSWERS instead of autocompleting
        |
        v
  REWARD MODEL        ->  bottle human taste into a score
  (comparisons)           (humans judge, not author)
        |
        v
  RLHF / PPO          ->  model chases the reward, on a leash,
  (or DPO, simpler)       becomes reliably helpful
        |
        v
  CONSTITUTIONAL / RLAIF -> scale and make values legible
        |
        v
  A HELPFUL ASSISTANT  ->  ChatGPT moment: the world can finally
                           just *talk* to AI
```

## The three ideas to carry forever

1. **Capability is not helpfulness.** Pre-training gives raw power; alignment gives usefulness. The 2022 revolution was alignment, not intelligence.
2. **The clever trick is preference, not perfection.** Humans can't author the best answer, but they can *judge*, and we turned that judgment into a learnable signal (reward model -> RL, or directly via DPO).
3. **Alignment is a steering wheel, not a guarantee.** It dramatically improves behavior but doesn't end hallucination, jailbreaks, sycophancy, or the deep question of *whose* values, and aligning superhuman systems remains unsolved.

## Why you, in 2026, must understand this

Every assistant, copilot, and agent your organization touches has been through some version of this pipeline. When a model is too cautious, too sycophantic, confidently wrong, or surprisingly jailbroken, you'll now understand *why*, it traces directly back to choices made in SFT, the reward model, and the alignment method. You're no longer a user of a black box. You can reason about its soul.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the synthesis, slow and deliberate. Re-draw the vertical pipeline; have the class call out each stage's job from memory. Land the three carry-forever ideas hard. Close by connecting behavior they've personally noticed (over-refusal, sycophancy, hallucination) back to specific pipeline stages, that's the moment the black box becomes transparent. Ask: 'Which model behavior that annoys you now makes sense?'
slides.marketConnection: Understanding this pipeline is, in 2026, the difference between a team that blames 'the AI' and one that can diagnose, select, and tune models intelligently, a real competitive edge in every AI-adopting organization.

slides.title: Quick Check: Did It Stick?
slides.body:
## Test yourself (answers below)

**1.** A raw base model, asked 'Write a poem about the ocean', might instead list more writing prompts. Why?
   a) It's broken
   b) It was trained to autocomplete internet text, not to follow instructions
   c) It hates poetry
   d) It needs a bigger GPU

**2.** The three stages of RLHF, in order, are:
   a) Reward model -> SFT -> PPO
   b) SFT -> Reward model -> RL (PPO)
   c) PPO -> DPO -> SFT
   d) Pre-training -> SFT -> deployment

**3.** The reward model exists because:
   a) Humans find it easier to *compare/rank* answers than to *author* perfect ones, and we need a scalable automated judge
   b) It makes the model bigger
   c) It replaces pre-training
   d) It eliminates the need for data

**4.** DPO became popular mainly because it:
   a) Requires a bigger reward model
   b) Achieves RLHF-like results without a separate reward model or unstable RL, much simpler
   c) Makes models less aligned
   d) Only works on closed models

**5.** The ChatGPT explosion (Nov 2022) was primarily driven by:
   a) A radically smarter underlying model than anything before
   b) Alignment + a free, simple chat interface making capability finally *accessible*
   c) A massive ad campaign
   d) New hardware

**6.** Which is NOT solved by alignment?
   a) The model tries to follow instructions
   b) Hallucination is completely eliminated
   c) Default tone is more helpful
   d) Overtly harmful requests are often refused

---

### Answers
**1: b** , autocomplete training, not instruction-following.
**2: b** , SFT, then reward model, then RL/PPO.
**3: a** , judging is easier than authoring; the RM is the scalable judge.
**4: b** , simpler: no separate reward model, no fragile RL loop.
**5: b** , accessibility via alignment, not raw new intelligence.
**6: b** , hallucination is *reduced, not eliminated*; alignment is a steering wheel, not a guarantee.

## Discussion
Think of a time a chatbot was sycophantic (agreed with you when you were wrong) or over-refused a harmless request. Trace it: which pipeline stage, SFT, reward model, or the alignment method, most likely produced that behavior, and how would you fix it?
slides.speakerNotes: Fast hands-up round to consolidate. Linger on Q5, it's the thesis of the whole day, and on Q6, because 'alignment doesn't solve everything' is the mature takeaway. For the discussion, push them to connect *observed behavior* to *pipeline cause*; that diagnostic skill is the real prize. Reward good 'why are the wrong answers wrong' reasoning.
slides.marketConnection: Diagnosing model behavior back to its training pipeline is exactly the literacy 2026 employers value, it turns vague AI frustration into actionable model selection and tuning decisions.

keyTakeaways: Capability is not helpfulness: pre-training produces a powerful but unhelpful 'internet-mind' that autocompletes instead of answering; alignment is what makes it a usable assistant.
Alignment aims for the 3 H's, helpful, harmless, honest, which are in constant tension; the craft is balancing them.
RLHF has three stages: SFT (learn from human-written ideal answers), a reward model (bottle human taste by having humans rank answers), and RL/PPO (chase the reward on a KL 'leash').

The core trick is preference, not perfection: humans judge answers (easy) instead of authoring perfect ones (hard), and that judgment becomes a learnable signal; DPO later achieved this far more simply.

The ChatGPT moment (Nov 30, 2022) changed the world not because the model was radically smarter, but because alignment plus a free, simple chat interface made capability finally accessible to everyone.

Alignment is a steering wheel, not a guarantee: it reduces but doesn't eliminate hallucination, jailbreaks, sycophancy, or the question of *whose* values, and aligning superhuman systems is still unsolved.
marketSnapshot2026: As of June 2026, the alignment recipe from this day is the standard finishing process for virtually every deployed model, open or closed. The field has largely shifted from complex PPO-based RLHF toward simpler, cheaper methods, DPO and its variants are the common default, and RLAIF / Constitutional approaches scale feedback using AI and written principles. Crucially, alignment has become *legible*: major labs publish model 'specs' and constitutions, turning 'what is this model supposed to do?' into something users and regulators can read and debate. The unsolved problems are now mainstream business and policy concerns: sycophancy and residual hallucination shape enterprise benchmarks and procurement, jailbreaks sustain a thriving red-teaming industry, and 'whose values / how censored should models be?' has become a competitive and geopolitical battleground. The deepest open frontier, scalable oversight of models smarter than their human supervisors, is now a funded research priority rather than a thought experiment.
homework: Two parts. (1) Reflection: write half a page on the claim 'the ChatGPT moment was about accessibility, not raw intelligence.' Do you agree? What evidence from your own use supports or challenges it? (2) Hands-on probing: in any chatbot you use, deliberately try to (a) catch it being sycophantic, ask it to agree with an obviously wrong statement and see if it caves; and (b) catch it over-refusing, find a harmless request it wrongly declines. For each, note which alignment-pipeline stage you think caused the behavior and how you'd tune it. Bring one surprising example to discuss.
nextDayTeaser: For years, the most powerful aligned models lived behind locked corporate doors, you could rent them by the API call, but never own them. Then, in early 2023, Meta's LLaMA weights slipped onto the internet, and the genie escaped the bottle for good. Tomorrow: the open-weight awakening, the Cambrian explosion of models you can download, run on your own laptop, and truly own, plus the fierce open-vs-closed debate now reshaping every enterprise's AI strategy.
